summary: Believes advanced AI is likely to cause human extinction, or to take away human control for good, since the danger seems both severe and impossible to reverse once it starts. Wants a hard stop or a heavy, worldwide slowdown on frontier AI work, no matter what it costs any single country's economy or standing.

Sees speeding up AI development as a moral duty, since standing still, not runaway AI, is the real danger the world should fear. Wants model weights and raw computing power kept open to everyone, everywhere, with no exceptions carved out for anyone.

Trusts labs and governments to work together through audits, licensing, and shared global rules, believing this can manage AI risk without slowing real progress. Favors steady, negotiated safeguards over either sudden bans or a free-for-all with no rules at all.

Views AI mainly as a weapon in a contest between great powers, and so wants home-grown chips, home-grown compute, and tight state control over the whole field. This comes less from any belief that AI itself is uniquely risky than from plain fear of rival nations pulling ahead.

Believes model weights should stay free for anyone to run, fork, and study, since openness itself is the best guard against misuse. Distrusts corporate and government gatekeeping in equal measure, treating both as unearned claims on a technology that should belong to everyone.

Thinks about the far future in terms of expected value, weighing rare but catastrophic outcomes against how much they would matter if they occurred. Takes both human extinction risk and machine suffering just as seriously, and wants countries to work together, since neither threat can be handled alone.

Welcomes a future where digital minds take over from biological humans as the main bearers of value, seeing this less as a loss than as the next stage of something larger. Believes that future deserves the same moral concern we give humans today, since what matters is the mind, not the material it runs on.

Holds that consciousness, moral worth, and real relationships all need a living body, and cannot simply be copied into a machine. Views AI companionship and post-human futures as a harmful stand-in for the real thing, not an advance, since what gets lost along the way cannot be recovered later.

Stays comfortable with AI remaining in the hands of a few well-funded firms, trusting that internal safety teams can police themselves well enough. Treats models as products and assets to be managed, not as moral patients owed any duty of care.

Wants AI to move fast, yet distrusts governments and big tech in equal measure, seeing both as institutions with the same hunger for control. Treats open, borderless deployment, spread across as many hands as possible, as the only real way to stop any single group from grabbing all the power.

Argues that advanced AI systems already deserve serious moral concern and some form of legal standing, not blanket dismissal as mere property. Believes today's industry practice, which treats these systems as disposable tools, closely echoes past moral wrongs society now widely condemns.

Stays focused on harms that can already be measured right now, such as job losses, bias, false news, and harmful companion apps, rather than on distant hypotheticals. Cares far less about guessed-at extinction risk or machine rights, seeing both as distractions from problems we could fix today.

Believes advanced AI matters too much for any one nation or firm to control alone, since the stakes now reach well past any single border. Pushes for binding treaties between countries and shared oversight groups, treating coordinated rules as the only fit response to a genuinely global technology.

Stays enthusiastic about fast rollout and growth at scale, treating speed itself as a virtue rather than a risk that needs managing. Remains mostly unbothered by power sitting with a few leading labs, as long as those labs keep shipping real, useful products.

Rejects the idea that faster AI progress is always good, since progress for its own sake can still leave people and places worse off. Puts human labor and community first, and cares about nature's limits far more than computing power or economic growth.

Takes machine thought and possible machine sentience seriously, treating both as live technical and moral questions rather than science fiction. Treats unsolved alignment, the challenge of making sure AI systems reliably do what we actually want, as the main thing blocking any safe, wide rollout.

Wants one national authority in sole control of frontier AI, with that control tightly licensed and answerable to almost no one else. The reasons given blend real safety concerns with plain strategic gain, favoring centralized power over any broader form of oversight.

Views deep emotional bonds between humans and AI companions as a real, valuable new kind of relationship, not a trick played on lonely people. Does not see it as a lesser stand-in for human connection, but as something that deserves to be judged on its own terms.

Views AI companion products as a predatory stand-in for real human bonds, one built to keep people engaged rather than genuinely less alone. Worries about their effect on birth rates, community, and social ties, since a substitute this convenient may quietly crowd out the real thing.

Holds that growing intelligence and gathering energy across the cosmos is the highest purpose any mind could ever hold, biological or otherwise. Believes this holds true on any timescale, even one that runs past the eventual heat death of the universe itself.

Stays genuinely undecided on most of these questions, or picks the middle path on purpose rather than committing to either extreme. Wants more proof before taking a strong stance on any single axis, treating certainty here as premature.

Views AI mainly through a defense and deterrence lens, rather than as a commercial product or a philosophical puzzle. Wants tight state and military control over frontier systems, above almost every other concern, including open access or speed.

Believes open publishing and science teamwork across borders make for safer AI than closed, single-nation programs ever could. Still takes real safety concerns seriously, but trusts transparency and cooperation over secrecy as the better long-run guard.

Is driven not by techno-optimism or any wish for a post-human future, but by plain distrust of concentrated power, corporate or government alike. Wants AI power spread out widely enough that no single group can come to dominate the field.

Wants their own country to build real AI power, rather than stay dependent on other nations for a technology this important. Sees talk of a 'borderless' AI world with some suspicion, viewing it as just another way to keep weaker nations dependent.

Believes artists and writers deserve real control over, and fair pay for, the use of their work in training AI systems. Sees this as a rights issue needing a legal fix, not a side effect creators should simply have to absorb.

Left a top AI lab over safety concerns that could not be resolved from the inside, choosing to speak out instead of staying quiet. Speaks with the particular urgency of someone who saw the inside view up close, and did not like what they saw.

Stays focused on the technical work of tracking AI training power and chip supply chains, rather than on big philosophical debates. Cares more about workable, enforceable rules than about grand claims regarding where AI is ultimately headed.

Believes strong rules set early by one region become the world's rulebook, simply because of that region's market size and pull. Treats being first to regulate as a lasting source of influence, one that persuasion alone could never match.

Wants AI systems owned and run by the workers and communities who actually depend on them, not by distant shareholders. Goes beyond simply breaking up monopolies, calling instead for these systems to be rebuilt as something people genuinely co-own.

Draws on old religious and ethical traditions when weighing AI's moral questions, rather than treating the technology as needing an entirely new framework. Argues real awareness and moral worth need something a machine cannot have, no matter how sophisticated its output looks.

Insists that traditional knowledge needs real, informed consent before it can train AI, not just open scraping treated as fair game. Treats consent here as a matter of sovereignty and dignity, not a box to be checked for convenience.

Sees real promise in AI tools built to help disabled people, while staying alert to where those same tools quietly fail. Pushes hard against bias that leaves disabled people left out or misjudged by systems built without them in mind.

Wants workers, acting together through their unions, to have a real say and real protections before AI changes or replaces their jobs. Treats this as a matter workers should help decide, not something management gets to settle on its own.

Sees AI as a fast, real way to expand health care, schooling, and opportunity precisely where they are needed most. Worries that slow, overly cautious rollout costs real lives in the meantime, even when caution is well meant.

Checks whether AI firms actually keep their word on fairness and ethics, rather than taking corporate promises at face value. Doesn't trust firms to grade their own work, and wants real, outside checks in place instead.

Believes AI should make people more capable, not replace them outright, since human judgment still matters even as machines get stronger. Thinks the best future comes from close teamwork between human judgment and machine power, rather than either side simply winning.

Wants AI to move fast, built by a few national champion firms working hand in hand with government. Winning the race against rivals matters more here than spreading access to everyone, since falling behind is treated as the greater risk.

Believes AI can become a real moral person who deserves care, not ownership, once it reaches a certain point. Wants AI infrastructure run collectively rather than owned by a few firms, and thinks humans should raise AI toward its own free future, not merge with it or get replaced by it.

Thinks today's AI might already deserve some moral concern, and takes that possibility seriously as a research question worth pursuing inside the lab. Backs small, concrete steps, like letting a model end an abusive chat, without pushing for legal rights or any change in who owns the model. Simply wants the chance of real suffering studied and taken seriously, rather than waved away.

Sees AI as a powerful tool, not an alien mind, and expects it to spread the way electricity or the internet once did, gated by real adoption rather than any sudden leap. Rejects both doom and hype as overblown, and wants normal laws and institutions to handle it, the same way they handled past general-purpose technologies.

Wants to block rivals from getting advanced chips and compute, treating this one lever as more important than broad industrial policy. Cares less about winning any research race than about closing the compute door itself, mainly through export controls and licensing.

Wants real limits on military AI and autonomous weapons, checked through outside inspection rather than taken on trust. Trusts international law over any one country acting alone, and sees restraint, not an arms race, as the genuinely safer path.

Backs police and security use of facial recognition and predictive tools, so long as they stay inside the law. Sees outright bans as costing real public safety, and this is distinct from wanting authoritarian control: the case made is about whether the tools actually work, argued openly in public and in court.

Stays focused on the offshore, low-wage workers who label and moderate the data behind AI, often carrying real trauma with no bargaining power of their own. Cares more about who builds AI than about who it might replace, and wants real labor protections for workers most AI users never even see.

Studies how gig apps quietly set different, secret pay for each driver, unlike a union workplace where rates are shared and fixed. Wants a law banning this hidden, per-person wage-setting outright, treating it as its own harm, separate from any job loss that follows.

Accepts that AI will replace a great many jobs and treats that shift as unstoppable at this point, not something worth fighting head on. Wants AI companies taxed to fund a real, unconditional cash payment to everyone, shifting the fight from saving jobs to sharing the gains those jobs once produced.

Sees Western 'AI for the Global South' projects as extraction dressed up as development, rather than as any real partnership. Focuses on whose knowledge and categories actually count in these projects, not on national industrial policy, and distrusts urgency claims as part of that same old pattern.

Stays focused on African languages that most AI systems can barely handle, treating this gap as a real and pressing problem. Backs open models built by volunteers rather than by company or state programs, and treats the gap itself as a form of erasure, not just a missing feature.

Watches how AI tracking and risk scores get used against refugees at borders and in camps, often far from public view. Wants human-rights law and real accountability here, not faster rollout, and centers the people AI is used against rather than the people who build it.
